CURRENT AFFAIRS.

In an article in this issue, Mr. John J. Hart suggests
the formation of an Irish International Industrial
League. The idea is an excellent one; the practical
point is, can it be worked? We want, an d have been
wanting for a long time a mere National Industrial
League, and it has not materialised yet. If a master-
ful man took up the Irish International Industrial
Leeague idea, he might cairy it to a considerable degree
of success. One great and: more or less (at present)
fundamental drawback to the growth of Irish Indus-
trial organizations is that so many of the industries—

“at present—are in the control of people w ho are what
we might call non-Irish where they are not posit: vely
anti-Irish. Combinations between a more or less Irish
national public and many more or less anti-Irish and
West British manufacturers are difficult; the two ele-
ments do not fuse easily. No doubt we will change all
that ; time is on the side of the Gael, and those who
will be absorbed by the Gael in Ireland.

reared at the cost of this poor country; 1ib dumps’
some of 1ts goods here against our own manufacturers,
and it sends a few paltry thousands of pounds to help
our Emmpees to “fight, for Ireland” on the floor ok the
British House of Commons.” If Ireland worked the
industrial possibilities of America as a customer for
Irish goods,.it would incidentally help to stem emigra-
tion, add to the wealth of the country, and render the
country in a bebber position to take over the xvl}ol,e
burden of the upkeep of the Emmpees or their equiva-
lents on or off “the floor.” We do not know whether
Mr. Hart is anxious to attempt the work of founding
the League he suggests. If he is, good luck to him;
and any help in reason that we can give him 1s at his

We thought that “The Church” was disestablished in
Ireland. But it would appear, according to Thom’s
Directory that we are wrong. Let us refer to a few in-
stances which go to show that “Thom’s” stall labours
under the impression that the Church of England’s
Faithful Garrison in Ireland is still ¢he Church of
Ireland. According to “Thom’s,” it would seem “The
Church” 1is the Episcopalian Protestant Chapel. We
find under Booterstown that “Its buildings are, a
handsome Church, with square towers and pinnacles,
and lofty spire, a large Roman Catholic Church . . .7
Under Bray we find: “The old Parish Church, now St.
Paul’'s Chapel of Ease, stands on the high ground, near
the bridge. The new Parish Church is in the upper
outskirts of the town. There are also two Roman
Catholic Churches " Under Clondalkin we
read, “The only public buildings are the Parish Church,
a small plain modern building, and a neat Roman
Catholic Church.” Under Crumlin, “The Church 1s a
plain building; the Roman Catholic Church a neat
edifice.’ Under Finglas, “The only public build-
ings are the Parish Church and the Roman
Catholic Chuwrch.”  Under Harold’s Cross “The
principal buildings are the Church, at the entrance
of Mount Jerome Cemetery, a spacious and elegant
structure, and the new and handsome Roman Catholic
Church of the Passionists.”  Under Irishtown, “Its
buildings are, the Church of St. Matthew, a Roman
Catholic Church 7 We have given sufficient
examples from this “saved” Thom’s Directory. Now
upon what principle are the Churches of England’s
Faithful Garrison in Ireland given precedence as against
the Churches of the vast majority of the historic Inish
people? Is “Thom’s” an Ascendancy directory? Why
is the ‘chapel of the “saved” designated as “The
Church”? Ias there been no Disestablishment?

The Irish Drapers Assistants’ Association have i1ssued

an appeal to the shopping public for early closing on

Saturday, in Dublin. In the course of this appeal we
read : —“The closing hours at present are as follows,
viz. :—The large, or what are considered the better-
class houses, close at: 6 o’clock p.m. all the week round,
most of the Grafton Street houses close at 4 o’c'ock p.m.
on Saturdays. But this does not mean that work then
ceases, as unpaid overtime, to the extent of two hours.
per day, is almost the general rule. In the medium
and small houses, the closing hours are:—7 to 8 ¢’clock
on five evenings of the week, and on Saturdays 10, 11,

and 12 o’clock. Our request is that the large houses

close ‘at 2 o'clock p.m. on Saturdays, and that the
medium and small houses close att 7 o’clock p.m. This,
we consider, would be a reasonable and an equitable
arrangement. We do not believe that our request.
would entail any inconvenience to the public. In the

case of the weal thy and leisured classes, it 1s, of course,

because of want of thought that they make their pur-

The case of the work-

5 _ THE LEADER.

gung-man paid on Friday or Saturday is different, and
ithe 7 o'clock closing will give him ample time to make
:all necessary purchases. The atmosphere of a Drapery
Establishment is close and confined, the tension on the
minds of those engaged therein is most severe, and it is
unnecessary for us to point out that working long
hours under such conditions has a most detrimental
«effect physically, mentally, and morally on those con-
-cerned. About forty per cent. of the Drapery em-
Jloyees of Dublin are girls and young women, and in
the shops which remain open till a late hour on Satur-
«day night, over eighty per cent. of the employeces are
females, nearly all of whom have to traverse long
«distances, in some cases three or four miles to their
themes umprotected at these unseasonable hours. As
4 result of this system of things, the amount of benefits
paid for sick claims to the members of our Association
15 nearly double the amount of similar benefits paid in
any other Society. We are never without cases in the
consumptive sanatoriums, and numbers of our body
are constantly recommended by their Medical Advisers
wo seek health in other countries, a recommendation
‘which many find it impossible to carry out.”  The
Drapers’ Assistants of Dublin have, we think our readers
will agree, a strong and reasonable case, and we hope
:that our readers will help them by all means in their
jpower 1n their attempt to lighten their over heavy lot.

Certainly the management at the Dripsey Woollen
Mills are not: Dark Brothers. They believe in looking
for business, and not in squatting in a cave waiting for
business to go on an exploring expedition to discover
them. We have before us a neat little booklet printed
in Cork, on Irish paper, setting forth many particulars
about their goods. They state that there are no unem-
ployed in Dripsey and no emigration from it:—“The
Dripsey Woollen Mills provide employment for all who
are able to work.  Almost every cottage within a
radius of two miles of Dripsey Mills is occupied by mill
«employés. When you support “Dripsey” you are help-
ing to keep those people employed, and you are en-
couraging us to do what we are doing—enlarging our
works and increasing our staff rapidly.” When the
pamphlet states that emigration carried off 58,308 sons
and daughters of Ireland in 1904, it apparently over-
states the case; the gross figure without allowance for
visitors and “returned Yanks” going back to America
‘was probably taken. Emigration is very large, but it
1s some consolation to know that a considerable re-
duction must be taken from the gross figure in order
to arrive at the actual drain from Ireland.

Needless to say the management of the Dripsey
‘Woollen Mills don’t appear before the public asking
customers for a grant in aid on every purchase they
make from the mills., They head a page of the booklet
with this pronouncement:—“Bear well in mind that
Irish manufacturers don’t ask for favouritism.” And
they also say:—“We confidently assertr that the goods
made at the Dripsey Woollen Mills are as artistic in
design, as tasteful in coloring, more durable in wear,
and very much more moderate in price than any wool-
len goods imported.” There is a business ring about
that and no trace of a whine for one penny more than
‘the goods are considered to be worth in the open
market ; of course as we have before stated, the Irish
manufacturers would be the first to repudiate doing
business on the grant-in-aid-from-their-customers prin,
ciple advocated by the green sentimentalists who tres-
pass on the Industrial Revival field. The proprietor
of the mills, Mr. A. O. Shaughnessy, 1t is stated in the
booklet—‘“Believes in the Irish Revival, and in order to
keep abreast of competition thousands of pounds have
been spent during the past few years in enlarging the

- mills and introducing the most modern machinery.”

There was an interesting discussion at the last fort-
nightly meeting of! the Skibbereen Industrial Develop-
ment Association during which some plain talk was in-
dulged in at the expense of the Dark Brothers. One
gentleman stated that he was told that a kig British soap

Feprusry 25, 1909.

firm were losing an immense amount of their Irish sozlj,l)-
trade owing to the growing demand for Irish soaps. The
discussion for a time turned on matches, and Mr. Wol_fe
said—“T believe Paterson’s matches, which are Il:lja,dellll
Dublin, are better than Bryant’s or anyone else’s. Th:e
““as good and as cheap principle” cropped up, and Mr.
Wolfe hit, off the situation very neatly when he}saﬂ,d——
“Make no sacrifice, but take an interest in bhe-.m.’ NO}V,
we consider that an excellent remark. “Taking an 1n-
terest”’ is in a way a voluntary protection, but it involves
no financial sacrifice, it is calculated to put no industry
into a false position. Every man can and ought to be
within reason, a propagandist for the support of Irish
goods that can hold their own in the market. The re-

sult of one man insisting on being served by his shop-

keeper with a certain Irish article with whose value he 1s
satisfied compels the shopkeeper to stock that article, and
as a consequence it is probably purchased by numbers of
others who are apathétic as between Irish and foreign
goods. The quality of Wexford agricultural machinery
was paid a tribute by Mr. G. L. Vickery. It was stated—
and it is the first we heard of it—that ploughs are made
in Skibbereen. Perhaps the maker only supplies local
needs ; if he wants a wider purchasing public he has cer-
tainly kept his industry very dark up to this; Mr.
O’Shea, a draper, referred to the one time famous
Kersey (Scotch) tweeds, and said that at the Dripsey
Mills a similar Irish tweed of Irish wool was fast cutting
out the others, and could be made cheaper.

One, John Flanagan, a Banbridge Bung, 1s a very
spirited man. It would appear that the Catholic Asso-
ciation of Belfast, subscribed to the Irish Parliamentary
Fund. This spirited Bung would not have the Irish
Party Fund contaminated with the foul money of the
Catholic Association of Belfast. In the course of a
published letter, this man John Klanagan, wrote:
I desire to emphasise my protest against the acceptance
of the Catholic Association’s subscription to the Parlia-
mentary Fund.” Was this the spirited John Flanagan
who subscribed £1 towards the purchase of fireworks,
cte., on the occasion of the King’'s Coronation? He says
in his letter thati “the universal feeling amongst na-
tionalists throughout Ulster” is that the money should
not be accepted. What an excellent judge this Bung
is of “the universal feeling” of Nationalists of Ulster !
We wonder what 1s the universal feeling of those
Nationalists towards a subscriber to a King’s Coronation
Fund ?

Last week again our contemporary, the organ of pigs,
eggs, poultry and other things, disappointed us; it had,
as far as we saw, nothing about the Manure War. Is
the “Wholesale” beaten? Surely the “Wholesale” if it
has anything further to say on the Manure War, suffers
from no lack of literary ability upon which it could
draw. The “Sober Nationalist” is an official of the
I.A.O.S., and he no doubt would be willing to rendex
service to the “Wholesale” and treat the manure ques-
tion practically, symbolically, or poetically, whichever
way Captain Loftus A. Bryan and his friends wished.
Any of the writers for a Celtic Christmas would, we are
sure, spin out a thing of beauty on the question, for the
colour of any artificial manure that we ever saw was of
a melancholy grey tint, and ought on that account lend
itself admirably to Celtic Christmas treatment. In the
last week’s issue of the Homestead there are notes on
plain sewing, on corns and warts, a review of a book of
one Celtic Christmas author by another Celtic Christmas

author, but nothing about the manure war. One of. -

the quotations from this book made by the reviewer in
the organ of co-operation is as follows:— |

“Oh ecarth! green wind-swept Eirinn, I would break
The tower of my soul’s initiate pride
For a gray field and a star-haunted lake,

And those west winds that roam the coun try side.”

Now a neat little thing about a gray field sprinkled
with imported United Alkali manure, and another field
fertilised with the manure of O’Keefe, Ltd., could be
turned out in verse, and Captain Loftus A. Bryan, of

. the militia, might recite it to the co-operative societies
“Wholesale” in the manuwre War. But
our contemporary, the organ of pigs, poetry, warts and
corns, 1s mum on the manwre war. Is the “Sober Na-
Lionalist” sulking in his tent that he does not come outs
. and smash O’Keefe, Ltd., with the butt-end of a Sym-
bol; or cannot some other poet: give O’'Keefe, Ltd., an
“opal hush” between the eyes. Perhaps one of the
writers for the comically-called “Irish National Theatre
Society” is writing a play on the Manure War, and
- may be it will be published in the next issue of the
Homestead.. We have not: given up hope yet that the
“Wholesale” is utterly silenced. Will it reply to our
article last we - Again we await the coming of the
next issue of our contemporary with interest.

Is it true that all the manure orders (if any) given to
the “Wholesale” by the Co-operative Societies will be
executed by Irish manufacturing firms, though the
orders may be nominally carried out by the ‘“Whole-
sale’s” British friend the United Alkali Co.? If so, is
1t not somewhat paradoxical for a “Co-operative” firm
to act 1in such a way as to call into being an eztra
middleman? We thought co-operative efforts aimed at
dispensing as much as possible with the private middle-
man. If it be true that the orders will be executed by
irish manufacturers, would it not be more in the
spirit of the co-operative ideal if societies dealt direct
with the Irish manufacturers rather than go a round-
about-way and send their orders to the “Wholesale”
that would send it over to a British firm that in turn
would send it over to O’Keefe’s, Ltd., or some other firm
to be actually carried out by them ? Perhaps Captain
Loftus A. Bryan would answer this question. Our space
In reason is at his disposal for a reply to this question,
and for a reply. to our article of last week.

Another week has passed, and the official organ of the
Gaelic League has had no reference to our criticisms.
Well, that is its business, and, as it is an official organ,
the business likewise of the Executive of the League. Tt
Lad a note in Irish, however, on the “as good and as
cheap” principle. We are not surprised to find that the

official organ does not take to it. A paper
that asks people to buy it because it is
the official organ, and  that, in  addition,

can dip its hand into the Language Fund and draw out
£611 7s.-on an eleven months’ working would, perhaps,
naturally enough believe in pampering Dark Brothers.
Our readers will remember that the official organ some
time ago stated that Ireland paid for her imports with
money ;- we corrected our spoon-fed contemporary at the
time, and. it took the correction lying down. Perhaps a
paper that said a thing of that sort is not worth noticing
on any question involving the elementary principles of
economics. However, the opinions of the official organ,
that cost the Language Fund £611 7s on an eleven
months’ working, have a special interest of their own.
We quote the Irish note in our contemporary : —

The following is a free translation of the note:— Re-
garding the question discussed by some of the speakers

. (at the recent Industrial Conference)—viz., whether wo

ought to reject Irish-made goods in the event of their
not, being as cheap as those of forei gn make, we are of

one mind with Alderman Cole. ILet, the Irish manufac. _

turer do his best, and, if he is assisted, there 1s no fear
but that he will before long be able to place his goods olrs:
the market as cheaply as his competitors ; but 1t 1s simply
foolish to say that we ought not to buy Irl,sqh-ma-anac-
tured articles on account of their being at present a little
dearer than those of foreign make. Those who advocate
such a policy have their eyes firmly fixed upon England,
although they themselves ave in blissful ignorance of the
fact.”

It 1s rather discouraging to have to take notice of this
sort, of editorial stuff in the official organ. The last part
is the real plum. If you hold an opinion on a business
matter—a matter of affairs—different from the sentimen-
talists who poke their brains into a domain of thought
where, to say the least, they are not at home—it 1s an
infallible sign that you have your eyes fixed on England !
This sort of thing drags controversy down to drivel, and
1t 1s painful to have to deal with it. As a matter of fa,cﬁ
a player to the gallery on the “as good and as cheap
side might, with equal force, say that the sentimentalists
had their eyes on Chamberlain, and the large English
party in favour of tariffs.

Let us point out that no one has ever said that a pri-
vate individual ought not pay more for Irish goods when
they are only equal value with foreign. For our own
part we very frequently give more than a preference to
Irish goods, and anyone who likes it, and can afford 1t,
can do likewise as often as they please. We say nothing
to stop them ; for we know very well that it is not neces-
sary to put on the brake whilst going up-hill. The
amount of money spent on Irish manufacture by those
who consciously pay more for things because they are—
or we are told they are—Irish, will neither make nor mar
the nation. At the best, the volume of trade done on
those principles would be but a bagatelle in comparison
with the trade of the country, and would have no na-
tional effect one way or the other.

There 1s no body of men who would repudiate the:
spoon-fed, voluntary  protection-” more earnestly than
the Irish manufacturers themselves There is not an
Irish manufacturer who would come out before the public
and say that the continuance of his industry depended
upon his receiving a grant in aid from everyone who
bought his goods. It is difficult to get money to capi-
balise an industrial venture in Ireland that has an excel-
lent chance of paying its way on the open market; we
wonder how much would the sentimentalists subscribe in:
the way of capital to a man who laid an industrial project
before them calculated to pay on the basis of the public
giving ten per cent. more for his output for a certain
number of years?

Perhaps, though we have touched on the matter so
often before, we may again make some general remarks.
on the whole question. We have consistently declined
to go largely into any controversy on legislative tariff
“protection” for the sound reason that Ireland has no
power in the matter. If Ireland had power to put up
tariffs, we would have clear and strong views on the
matter, and we would slate our views with all the vi gour
at our command. Bubt for Ireland tariff “protection’
is merely academic, with the addition that a great deal
of useless and mischievous heat and bad feeling might
be stirred up if the useless—to Ireland—question were
discussed. The Unites States, one of the most, if not
the most, resourceful and wealthy countries in the world,
is a great and strong industrial entity, and it is walled
round by tariffs—a curlous commentary on those senti-
mentalists who are fond of talking of “protecting”
young industries. In the United States the tariffs
don’t appear to dwindle away with the years. Of course
those who know the A B O of the matter know that
tariffs, like bung licenses, tend to create vested in-
terests, and once granted, so great is the accretion that

may' grow around them that a  proposal
to take them away mmght become a very
difficult and formidable problem indeed. But

there is one thing about legislative tariffs—they are

compulsory 'all round; the meanest and most miserly
man 1n the country is as much subject to them as the
most generous; the purchasing public in legislatively
“protected” countries buy in the cheapest markets open
to them as keenly as the most: shrewd Manchester man,
or the most shrewd members of the Irish “patriotic”
public. The manufacturers can make close and reliable
calculations; matters of rallway frei ghts, methods of
business, labour-saving machinery are in America not.
withstanding its “tariffs” subject to the check and stimu-
lus of competition.

Now the “voluntary” protection of a few hair-brai ned
sentimentalists of this country is not a, parallel of the
compulsory legislative tariffs of “‘protection” countries.
The ex-Tin Pike organ, now the organ of the King’s
Most Excellent Majesty, the Lords and Commons of Ire
land, having seen an almost perfect parallel between
Hungary and Ireland, would probably see a parallel in
the letter X ; and its poor “confrere” the official orcan
of the Gaelic League, is not to be taken too sea'iogsly
on economics. The Lord Chancellor might go to the
official editor on an important point of law, but we
would not think of consulting him on a question in-
volving a mastery of the A B C of economics.

There 1s radical difference between compulsion and
that which is voluntary. Now if we had based the
Irish Industrial Revival on the principle of buying
over-priced stuff because it was Irish, or because the
shopman told you it was Irish, there would have been
no Irish Industrial Revival such as we have witnessed
as the outcome of the LEADER’s propaganda. The
people would not have heeded us; the manufacturers
would have shrugged their shoulders. In legislatively
“protected” countries, at least, you have a check; vol-
untary “protection” is a leap in the dark. Supposing
that a man produced turnips. Let us say that he paid
a great deal too much to an absentee landlord for his
land; that he had two maiden aunts who should be
earning their own living, but whom he had to keep as
they were too genteel to be anything short of ladies’
maids, for which office they were unfitted; supposing
that he himself prefers reading the raimeis speeches in
the Freeman’s Journal, or being a member of the Cor-
poration to minding his business, suppose that he does
not believe in manuring his land, and that the railway
company asks him double what is equitable for carrying
his turnips to market, supposing that his son, instead
of footing turf, becomes a B.L., and has to be supported
as an expensive digesting machine operating in the
library of the Four Courts—now that man raises a field
of turnips. He cannot meet all the expenses of his
B.L. son, his maiden aunts, his excessive railway
freights, etc., and sell his Irish turnips at market value.
He, being a shrewd man, goes to Alderman Cole and
says—“These are Irish-made turnips,” the Alderman
would naturally ask the price.  Well,” the man might

~say, “I can’t sell them at a reasonable profit less than

a shilling a-piece.” For it must be remembered that
the man paid too much for railway freights and for his
land, and that he had to keep two maiden aunts, and
2 B.L. son without briefs. Being Irish turnips the
Alderman would buy a thousand turnips for £50, even
though he might be able to get foul, nefarious, im-
ported Saxon turnips for a farthing a-piece.  The
Alderman would want, we suppose, at least 25 per
cent. for the handling of them, so that nis price to cus-
tomers would be 1s. 3d. per turnip. The problem then
would be to avoid the foul “as good and as cheap”
scoundrels, and unload the turnips on the green sen-

timentalists. He would naturally offer some turnips to

the Editor of the official organ. ‘“What are you asking

Well, the above is an exaggerated picture, of course;
bub it sets the sentimentalists in a true light. -We must
have checks; it won't do to cover up railway “ nation-
killing,” antediluvian methods, the Irish striking man,
the lazy Dark Brothers, and the various other elements
that may go to inflate the price of Irish-manufactured
goods with an ointment of tem or twenty per cent. that
will leave free play to the evils covered up to work
their way under the skin to the permanent detriment of
the country.

“ As good and as cheap ” is the only sound, statesman-
like, general principle to advocate in Ireland. It 1s a
general principle. We have no objection to people who
wish to do so, violating it; very few people will violate
it. In exceptional cases where people have an industry
under their eyes, where they have reason for believing
that the industry is carried on to the best of the ability
of all concerned, they can—in odd cases of that kind—
give a preference wath thewr eyes open if they like—a
different thing from going into a shop ready to pay extra
for some article the shopman tells you is Irish, and of
the making, cost of production, and so forth, of which
you are absolutely in the dark.

Now we are to take it that the official view of the
Gaelic League, as voiced by its official organ that is so
valuable, that the Language Fund paid it over £611 Ts.
on an eleven months’ working is against the “ as good and
as cheap ” fools like ourselves. As members of the Gae-
lic League we enter our protest. Who asked the official
organ of the Gaelic L.eague to pronounce an official Gaelic
League opinion on a matter of affairsinvolving a mastery
of the A B C of economics? As the Gaelic League is non-
sectarian and non-political, why should its official organ
be allowed to express an official opinion on a question of
economic policy upon which, no doubt, members of the
League have individually very strong and very different
views? Many Gaelic Leaguers might well say—Save us
from the official organ.

Of course, the official organ’s “confrere,” the ex-Tin
Pike Organ, now our semi-Hungarian contemporary, the
King’s Most Excellent Majesty, the Lords and Commons
of Ireland paper, is death on the “as good and as cheap”
principle. If a manufacturer did not believe in the as
good and as cheap ” principle, one would not be inclined
to think that the quality of his goods were above sus-
picion like Cesar’s wife ; and there are some papers, like
the official organ, to which the principle of paying a penny
for a penny’s worth would spell death. The organ of the
King's Most Excellent Majesty, the Lords and Commons
of Ireland, says:—* The silly speech of the meeting was
contributed by the Rev. Father Dowling, C.M. Father
Dowling alleged that the Cork Industrial Development
Assocration held to the English ¢ as good and as cheap’
shibboleth, by which Father Dowling himself, obsessed
by the English mind, swears. It is almost unnecessary
to say that the Cork Association does no thing of the
kind. Itis composed of intelligent men.”” Perh aps now
that the ex-Tin Pike Organ has done a somersault , and
1s the organ of the King’'s Most Excellent Majesty, the
Lords and Commons of Ireland, it m ay have become
obsessed by the mind of the Right Hon. Joseph Chamber-

lain, Arch-Imperialist and Arch-Enemy of the “as good

and as cheap” twaddle!

FATHER DOWLING ON EXPORTS AND IMPORTS.

DEar Sir,—Referring to the figures quoted by me at
the Dublin Industrial Conference, I wish to set myself
right with your readers. The time of the Conference was
very valuable, and hence I did not like to go into details
to any extent. Consequently I simply gave the figures of
trade with the outside world just as I should give the
figures for Denmark, Belgium, or Switzerland. These
figures for 1902, in round numbers, will be found in the
Statesman’s Year Book, 1904, page 84.As to the trade of
Ireland with England, I did not touch on that because it
1s extremely hard to find out exact information. As your
readers are aware there is no distinct report issued as to
trade between Ireland and England. In the Depart-
ment’s Journal, Vol. IT., No. 3, page 43, the exports to
England for 1893 are given at about £25,000,000, and
But
these import figures are very delusive. We all know that
enormous quantities of goods come from the foreigner
via England. Then, again, any information I could getion
this head would only strengthen my case, because, taking
the figures as they stand above we should be in a very
good condition if our exports were £25,000,000 and our
imports £11,000,000 ; but we pay away that £11,000,000
to the foreigner for what. we could easily produce at home,
and, in some instances, are actually producing. In view
of the severe handicap under which Ireland competes
with other nations in supply England, preferential rail-
way rates, etc., I think that a good test of how we really

~stand with the world is the same as we apply to other

lands—namely, what business they are doing with the
world outside.—Yours truly,

SIR HORACE PLUNKETT'S BOOK.

SOME INDUSTRIES PROMOTED BY PRIESTS.

[ HAVE said that the promotion of political of indus-
trial movements is not the business of a priest.
‘Only special circumstances can make such works incum-
bent on him, and in those cases they are not a dubty of
Justice, but of charity. When priests in Ireland have,
for reasons which seem good to themselves, held aloof
from such movements they have been accused of disre-
garding the temporal interest of their people; when they
h_a.ve taken an active part in politics they have been some-
times, and by some persons, told that they have been
‘going beyond the boundary of their rights, and when they
have interested themselves in industrial work, they have
been sometimes reminded that they are not men of busi-
ness; from which, it seems clear to me, that the presence
-of the priest in those movements is desiderated by some
merely as a sort of lever, as an instrument to be used or
rejected according as he serves or impedes their purpose.
‘The priests of Ireland have never, and if I know them
rightly they never will, consent to be the shuttlecocks of
any party in the affairs of their country. They bear
civil burdens, and they claim civil rights. When, there-
fore, they interest themselves in political or industrial
work they do so without saying ““ by your leave” to any-
body. I assume, as a matter of course, that priests are
not; business men. Their training and life do not tend to
habits of business in the technical sense of that expres-
sion. Yet some of them show remarkable business gifts
and habits, and carry business transactions through with
unqualified success. On the other hand, men who are
supposed to be specialists not unfrequently show them-
selves remarkable only for causing confusion or failure in
business transactions which they undertake. I now
make a short review of some industrial works promoted
by priests in Ireland ; undertaken because of their neces-
sity, and because those did not initiate them who might
have more fittingly done so.

In Galway, the Most Rev. Dr. M‘Cormack and Father
Dooley started a woollen industry. They provided, or
became Tesponsible for, more than half the capital, which
was over £10,000. The concern is now worth more than
double that amount after paying regularly a dividend of
71 per cent. It hasnever received either money or coun-
sel from the Department, and yet it has been made a
very successful undertaking, which 1s, in a great mea-
sure, due to the ability ard care of the manager, who has

FELLOWS SUPPLIED.

HIS article is written in the honest belief that the
best way to get a scheme accepted is to have it
worked out in detail ; I shall therefore ask the ladies and
gentlemen mentioned below to take that as my excuse for
making free with their names and bringing forth their
abilities into the light of day. But the article is also
written with the more immediate purpose of combating
an utterly absurd delusion as to our own incapacity,
which, like so many other such delusions, Catholics
seem only too ready to indulge. I confess I have found
1t a little difficult to listen with patience to the state-
ment so often put forward, that one of the difficulties
Catholics labour under in regard to starting an Uni-
versity or University College, is that they have no body
of properly qualified teachers to man it. It is no doubt
commonly added, by way of consolation,that such a body
may eventually be developed or the like. Even Mr.
Dillon in his recent address, only argued that there
were a certaln number of teachers available. Whilst
Mr. Stephen Gwynn, in his Dublin lecture, seemed to
bhink the Catholic teaching faculty so negligible that
he seriously proposed thati they should all be retired on
pension. ,

Now the truth is that the statement that we have no
body of qualified University teachers in Irveland, is
about, as true as a statement that we have no body of
qualified Catholic clergymen. Tt is quite on a par
with the fiction about the want of qualified Catholics
for railways and other positions. My purpose in this
article 1s to show that were we called on of a Monday,
to man our new college with a body of fellows equal in
number and ability to the fellows of Trinity College,
we could have ready to lecture on the We dnesday such
a body amply qualified and chosen by the same test as

organizations are making preparations for the due and

proper observance of the holiday. It is, however,
greatly to be regretted that up to the present no
organized movement seems to have been attempted this
year with a view to the closing of the Dublin public-
houses on the 17th March. Public opinion expressed
itself 1n favour of their being closed two years ago when
the holiday was first established. More than half of
them within the Dublin Metropolitan Police Areca closed
on that occasion. Last year fewer closed, and, if
something be not done to prevent such a disgrace, fewer
still will close this year. Has public opinion changed?
Has it become less strong upon the subject? If it has,
should not an attempt be made to rouse it? If it has
?Ot" ;should not an attempt be made to give expression
0 1t¢

As chairman of the National Holiday Committee
which organized the first successful celebration in 1903,
in Dublin, T am fully aware of the great difficulty of
getting the licensed trade to fall in with other trades
by closing their establishments. The difficulty in 1903
was at least as great as the difficulty is to-day. In my
opinion 1t was greater, because amidst the enormous
amount of work that had to be done in connection with
the general establishment of the holiday, we were unab'e
to concentrate our energies upon it; besides which the
whole undertaking was then in its initial stage and
publicans had the excuse that there was no precedent
for them.  To-day they have no such excuse, and
although difficulties do exist they are such as could he
overcome 1f properly attacked. The regrettable feature

1s, there seems likely to be no effort to tackle them at

all. '

Outside of those interested in the drink trade itself,
few people regard as at all satisfactory the reasons given
by the trade why it should not observe St. Patrick’s
Day as a holiday. Press and public alike were unani-
mous in condemning the attitude taken up by the

“ WAR

Remember the glories of

brave,

- FEBRUARY 25, 1905.

Vintners’' Association two years ago, as set forth in the
manifesto of its Secretary, Mr. Russell, issued in answer
to the Holiday Committee’s request to advise a general
closing. Nothing but a selfish regard for their own
interests decided the adoption of that attitude, and
nothing bub a selfish regard for their own interests will
force its abandonment. When Mr. Bung is made to
realize—by one means or another—that he would _los;e
more than he would gain by opening on St. Patrick’s
Day, then, and only then, will he consenv to close. The
history of the Holiday movement in Dublin, short as
it is, leaves little room for any other conclusion than
this. |

In 1903, the measures adopted to induce the publi-
cans to close were mainly peaceable measures. I say
“mainly” because there was undoubtedly a flavour of
persuasion—‘gentle”’ or otherwise—about some of them.
When a South American Republic becomes refractory,
there is sometimes a “movement”’ of the United States’
fleet. So when argument seemed likely to fail with the
publicans two years ago, a demonstration of force was
made. It was purely “unofficial,” no doubt, but 1t had
its effect, and between Demonstration Sunday and the
National Holiday very large numbers of the trade an-
nounced their intention of closing, though they had pre-
viously declined to do so. I am strongly of the opinion
